title: 是什么阻碍了四天工作制？
author: Kelsey Hansen
content:
Organizational transformation

by Kelsey Hansen

April 9, 2026
HBR Staff/Streamline
  • Post

Summary.   

If the four-day workweek creates happier employees without a loss in productivity, why isn’t it more common in the United States? To answer this question, we asked our audience about what holds their companies back from adopting a shorter workweek,
Leer en españolLer em português
  • Post

The four-day workweek is far from widespread in the United States. Yet, its proponents argue that moving away from the five-day workweek is not just good for employee satisfaction and wellbeing; it can address some of the modern workplace’s most pervasive issues, like employee engagement, without leading to a drop in productivity. And as AI gets better and better at boosting efficiency, implementing a shorter workweek may become even more of a realistic option. Just this week, OpenAI published a policy paper suggesting companies pilot a four-day workweek as an “efficiency dividend” to give time back to workers.

This is the case that authors Jared Lindzon and Joe O’Connor make in their new book, Do More in Four: Why It’s Time for a Shorter Workweek. Lindzon is a journalist and O’Connor designs and leads four-day-workweek pilots around the globe.

To understand the resistance to a shorter workweek, we asked the HBR audience what was stopping their companies from adopting it—and any other questions they had about it. We then shared the (edited) questions with Lindzon and O’Connor for their insights, which are below. You can also check out their book for a deeper dive.

What are the direct and indirect benefits of the four-day workweek? 

The benefits are many, and range from individual, organizational, and societal.

At the individual level, those who switch to a four-day workweek often describe the extra day off from work as life changing, whether they’re using the time to volunteer, travel, be with family, or simply tackle an overflowing to-do list. It often offers a balance many feel is lacking.

At the organizational level, the three primary benefits reported by employers are: the creation of a compelling employee value proposition to reduce turnover and attract top talent; reduced burnout and improved wellbeing; and the creation of a powerful incentive to engage employees to make other big productivity-boosting changes, like implementing new processes and tools, including AI.

At the societal level, the four-day workweek has been proven to provide environmental benefits, improve workplace gender balance, and increase things like volunteerism and community engagement.

Are companies that adopt the four-day workweek more productive? 

Organizations that use the four-day workweek as a big incentive to implement big changes often see big productivity gains, despite the reduced schedule.

If you do nothing other than remove one day from the calendar it’s likely that productivity will plummet. If, however, you offer the four-day workweek as a reward for finding organizational efficiencies, implementing new processes, tools and technologies and focusing everyone’s time and energy on key business objectives, it can be a huge productivity booster.

That is why we believe the four-day workweek should be utilized as an operational effectiveness tool with a built-in incentive.

With fewer working days, how do you offer the same level of service to clients and customers? And how do you stay competitive when other companies work five days a week?

Our book shares the stories of many organizations that have taken creative approaches to solving that very problem. We actually have an entire chapter titled “But what about my clients?”

One organization created a “decision tree” that dictated when a client request warrants immediate action, and when it could be pushed until the next business day. Another designates one staff member to be on call from each team on that day off on a rotating schedule, so once a quarter each staff member needs to remain within 10 minutes of a keyboard in case a client calls. Some also stagger schedules so that each staff member only works four days but the business is open for five, which often requires moving to more shared emails and client management practices.

Overall, however, we find that most organizations find this to be a much bigger problem in theory than in practice. The reality is that we live in a world where work can effectively follow us anywhere. If an urgent customer requirement arises on that fifth day, most handle it in the same way they would handle it on an evening or weekend today.

Does coordination and communication get more difficult in a four-day workweek model?

On the contrary; when done right, the four-day workweek provides an opportunity for organizations to better manage how they collaborate and coordinate work.

Yes, the change could be disruptive to business communication practices; however, that fear is precisely what motivates organizations to get more serious and intentional about internal communication prior to making the switch.

Undertaking that kind of change can be daunting, and time consuming, which is why such efforts tend to fall to the bottom of everyone’s priority list. However, the prospect of a shorter workweek provides the necessary motivation to encourage that kind of change.

My colleagues who have a four-day workweek end up working on the fifth day just to keep up with urgent tasks. Is that just part of the model, or can you avoid work spilling over into the fifth day? And does management also need to work a fifth day to pick up the slack?

When an organization switches to a four-day workweek, management is encouraged to do the same, however it’s important to acknowledge that many managers are starting from a different baseline.

Middle managers in particular are often stretched so thin, and their days are so filled with managerial responsibilities, that they can only find the time to focus on their individual work on evenings and weekends.

Often we find that the switch to a four-day workweek gives managers who used to work six or seven days a week a proper weekend break, as they have one full day to dedicate to undisrupted, heads down work.

In an ideal world, management gets to enjoy the same schedule as everyone else, but we know that is not often the case—whether their organization operates four days a week or five.

If my company adopts the four-day workweek, does that mean everyone still works 40+ hours, just over four days? 

A shorter workweek is not one-size-fits-all, and different organizations will craft their four-day schedules in ways that best suit their needs.

However, the approach we strongly advocate for—and have seen the greatest success with—involves a genuine reduction in working time, such as four eight-hour days (or 32 hours per week), as opposed to a compressed workweek. This acts as a forcing function for genuine transformation in how work gets done rather than merely a schedule change.

As an aside, some organizations choose to start with four 10-hour days on their way to a four-day workweek of 8-hour days.

If people end up working 32 hours each week, do salaries and paid time off remain the same?

Salaries often remain the same, and the number of PTO weeks remains the same, though many organizations that make the switch also reduce the total number of PTO days on a pro-rata basis to reflect the new schedule.

For example, if a worker previously had four weeks of vacation per year, that is the equivalent of 20 days on a typical five-day schedule, but only 16 days if each of those four weeks is one day shorter.

Is there an increased labor cost associated with the four-day workweek?

Most of the organizations we have worked with and studied, including those documented in the book, have achieved a shorter workweek without an increase in employee headcount.

The expectation is to deliver the same outcomes in the condensed timeframe, through a process of redesign rather than intensification.

In fact, many organizations have realized significant second-order cost benefits to a shorter workweek. They include reduced recruitment, training, and transition costs thanks to the improved retention; reduced sick leave and absenteeism; and long-term pay restraint due to the competitive differentiation of a shorter workweek.

Does the four-day workweek increase employee entitlement? 

When designed and implemented thoughtfully, a shorter workweek can act as a powerful driver of accountability.

We recommend positioning the shorter workweek not as an entitlement but as an earned reward, which is contingent on clearly defined performance expectations and business outcomes.

Given the powerful, life-changing nature of the incentive, this can create significant peer-to-peer accountability, and a culture of continuous improvement in order to sustain the benefit.

Our ecosystem thinks in terms of hours and not in goals. How do I encourage a large-scale mindset shift?

You’ve actually identified the very reason why the four-day workweek is such a powerful tool.

These changes are necessary, and can unlock immense value, but they’re extremely difficult to implement. Change management is hard, and it only takes a few resistors to tank major productivity initiatives.

You’ve also pointed out how so many of our workplace systems are designed to optimize presence and visibility over productivity and real results, and it takes a big incentive to force people into an entirely different way of thinking.

We believe that the four-day workweek is one of—if not the only—sufficiently significant employee benefit that cannot just inspire such a dramatic change to the way things have always been done, but to do so with enthusiastic employee buy-in.

Which organizational processes would actually need to be redesigned—not just compressed—for a four-day workweek to work sustainably?

The changes that are necessary are often unique to an organization’s context, but some of the most common areas of reinvention include:

  • Overhauling meeting culture and embracing async-first communications
  • Eliminating distractions and creating a more focused, intentional work environment
  • Adopting AI and productivity-boosting technologies
  • Improving clarity and reducing bureaucracy in decision-making
  • Clarifying definitions and processes for escalations and emergencies
  • Creating greater alignment between business goals, ROI, and team/individual priorities
  • Promoting personal productivity practices like giving every hour in the workday a clear purpose, and intentionally designing your workday to align with your personal performance and energy patterns

How can I propose this to my organization?

Our book has an entire chapter dedicated to equipping staff with the tools they need to make a strong case for the four-day workweek at their organization.

Some of the advice we offer includes:

  • Identify which potential benefits of the four-day workweek are most likely to appeal to your decision makers: typically either employee attraction and retention, wellbeing and mental health, or process improvement and tech adoption.
  • Come equipped with data and case studies that demonstrate the opportunity to achieve those potential benefits in ways that would otherwise be more difficult, costly, time consuming, or entirely unachievable.
  • Speak with colleagues across the organization to understand the challenges in various roles and departments.
  • Work with that group to find solutions to the various potential obstacles you’ve identified.
  • Ensure that you’re framing the four-day workweek as an incentive for improving business performance, not an employee perk or entitlement.
  • Acknowledge that it’s a gradual process and expect to start small, such as with a summer Friday program or even an information session to gradually build toward a pilot.

Does the four-day workweek work in every industry—even consulting?

The critical mass of four-day workweek adoption to date has been mostly concentrated in knowledge work, for some obvious reasons.

In these organizations there is often significant low-hanging fruit as far as time saving opportunities, particularly when it comes to the inordinate amount of time spent on coordination and communication. Coupled with the more indirect relationship between hours and outcomes, the transition tends to be more straightforward than in other sectors.

However, while other sectors pose greater complexity, we can now point to case studies in almost every industry, from manufacturing to healthcare to skilled trades and everything in between.

In the book, we include success stories from a global consumer goods behemoth, a boutique architecture and design firm, a family law firm, a SaaS company, a non-profit and a creative marketing agency.

In professional services like consulting, particularly in more traditional firms, the billable hour often creates the biggest obstacle.

AI presents an interesting disruption to this traditional model given that future value creation will likely be a combination of human labor and “digital labor.”

For the meantime, we have seen firms overcome this obstacle either by shifting wholly or partly to value-based or fixed-fee pricing, by finding sufficient efficiencies in non-billable overheads to maintain billable hours, or some combination of both.

However, the existing model assumes a “utilization rate” that is realistically achievable in a standard workweek, which is often not the case!

While the practices and principles in our book might not help someone who routinely works 55- to 60-hour weeks achieve a four-day workweek, it can help them find ways to make it home in time for dinner on a more regular basis. The starting point matters, and our goal is to move the needle in the right direction and create a more balanced future of work for all.

We are not arguing that a four-day workweek will work for every firm in every industry, nor that it makes sense for every culture and every operational context right now.

We do believe, however, that much like remote work, it can work in a larger proportion of scenarios than was previously assumed, and can deliver significant business benefits to organizations of all shapes and sizes, when executed correctly.

aiSummary: 本文探讨了推行四天工作制的障碍，并指出当将其作为一种运营效率工具而非简单的日程调整来实施时，它能显著提升生产力和员工福祉。
oneSentenceSummary: 本文探讨了推行四天工作制的障碍，并指出当将其作为一种运营效率工具而非简单的日程调整来实施时，它能显著提升生产力和员工福祉。
summary: 本文探讨了为何四天工作制尽管潜力巨大，但在美国仍未普及。作者借鉴了专家 Jared Lindzon 和 Joe O'Connor 的见解，强调成功的转型需要从根本上重新设计工作流程——例如采用异步沟通、利用 AI 以及关注产出而非工时——而不是仅仅将五天的工作量压缩到四天。文章将缩短工作周视为组织效率的“应得奖励”，并突出了其在提高人才留存率、减少职业倦怠以及培养问责文化方面的作用。

mainPoints.point: 四天工作制应被视为一种运营效率工具。
mainPoints.explanation: 公司不应仅仅是减少一天工作时间，而应将缩短的日程作为一种激励机制，以消除官僚主义浪费、优化流程并采用 AI 等新技术，从而维持或提高产出。

mainPoints.point: 成功的实施需要从“基于出勤”向“基于产出”的工作模式转变。
mainPoints.explanation: 组织必须摒弃以工时衡量绩效的做法，转而关注明确的业务目标，这需要思维模式的重大转变和有意识的变革管理。

mainPoints.point: 解决客户和沟通方面的顾虑是设计问题，而非无法实现。
mainPoints.explanation: 企业可以通过错峰排班、轮换值班人员以及采用异步优先的沟通方式来维持服务水平，这证明了对客户可用性的担忧往往是理论上的，而非实践中的障碍。

keyQuotes: 如果你只是简单地从日历中删掉一天，生产力很可能会大幅下降。
四天工作制应被用作一种带有内置激励机制的运营效率工具。
缩短工作周并非“一刀切”，不同的组织会根据自身需求定制最适合的四天工作日程。
我们建议不要将缩短工作周视为一种权利，而应将其视为一种应得的奖励，这取决于明确定义的绩效预期和业务成果。
